speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. This is tape 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, in-- in Zaporozh'e, Ukraine-- that's a part of the Soviet Union. The-- at that time, you know, the Polish communists started to do some political work. And they used to keep us-- call us to-- for a conference, talk about communism, you know. And-- and to be-- because we were on the Soviet side, yeah. And I was not-- I was not active. I was never active. And came-- then came the time that we're going-- as I said, we're going back to Poland. And gave us some food on the way. And, uh-- I have to remind myself. Yeah-- they kept us at the border for a long period of time. Because they wanted-- the Soviets are very-- in this respect, they are very careful and very dangerous. And they kept-- of course, I have this kind of-- I still have the registered paper, which I got. It was in two parts. One was written-- printed in Russian language, and one part in Polish. The Polish part, I still have it. I believe I showed it to you when we first met. And they were-- took a few hours. And we-- and then on the border, I could see all those cars from Poland going into the Soviet Union, with that, uh-- what you-- coal, coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coal, yes. Polish-- to burn!

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm, coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the hundreds-- so many. Because they used to take out from Poland whatever they could. And then-- and at that place, it was just those coal we could see. And then we came-- they took us to Silesia. This was a part which-- which they-- make it as part of Poland. Actually, it was a part of Germany. But according to the Yalta agreement with-- with President Roosevelt and Churchill, the-- the Soviet Union got the eastern part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for this eastern part of Poland, they gave to the Polacks the part of-- of Germany, like Silesia and Pomerania, close to the-- to the Baltic Sea. Yeah. And over there, they would kept us a little bit in that-- I arrived to Waldenburg, which is-- should I spell it? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's W-A-L-D-E-N-B-U-R-G. And, they-- little by little, they put us into the German families. I was by myself. I was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and it was just a guy who used to work on the-- in those-- coal. How you call it? A guy who works-- Mine worker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miner, yeah. And I came to a family for a mine worker, and to stay there. And I used to be there with them. Until a few weeks, until they gave me some apartment. Not for myself-- with another fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the attitude of this German family taking in a Jewish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't-- they couldn't say anything. They tried to be very nice, because they were afraid that something will happen to them. You know, it was not like you live here in the United States, that you want to say anything. You can see-- then you see what happened in-- in Oklahoma City. It's-- people were rather afraid of-- they were happy that they got somebody and-- taking care of them. I remember I brought a little bit of flour, dark flour. You never see this in this country. Even this-- this guy, he couldn't believe it. He was a worker, a mine worker, and you have this white flour. And-- and we came, you know, very bad-- very bad conditions. And-- and then, of course, they used to have those Jewish committees. Committee. And you can see on the-- on that, my ID card, which I got from-- they gave me a coat. They put down I got the coat. I got some money-- everything is written. I-- I can show. I believe I gave it to you. And, of course, I got a job. Again, as a bookkeeper for the-- but at that time, it was no different whether a Jew or not Jew. And I used to get a job for the city. Now anyway, everything belonged to the government. Because it-- it was a socialist-- now, in the beginning, it was not so. Beginning, still we had some private stores. But my job was at the-- for the city. And we worked on the real-- in the properties-- real properties. And I took care on the bookkeeping-- part of the bookkeeping of the-- of those properties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was finding these jobs for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government. Actually, not the government-- the Jewish organizations. They used to get-- try to get, you know, those people who came from the Soviet Union, to take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me get a little perspective. Liberation was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Liberation was, um-- the liberation was actually from Komi, when the war broke out-- two months or three months after the war broke out, between the Soviet Union and the German-- and Hitler's army. This was the Liberation already in Komi. But-- but the whole story is what I said-- I was as a free man. But still in very, very poor conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after Liberation was it that you wound up in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In where? In Asia?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Silesia. It's about-- '41? About '40-- three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure--

speaker: interviewer
text: It took three and a half years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to be in-- in different-- in different conditions. We used to be in the Soviet Union. You know, the travelling-- the travelling. That was the wartime. And the-- they needed the railroad. And they used to keep us on the [? sites ?] for days-- for days. But we were free. We were not locked in those-- in those cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you found out anything about your family in those three and half years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we found out just what's going on. We found out, let's say, about that Hitler is, you know, killing those Jews, and about the Holo-- nothing-- some little news. Not too many. Not too much. Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the three and a half years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- yes. We found out about what was going on. What actually was-- what happened. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you have a job as a bookkeeper in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, yeah, I'm working there. I am in an apartment. I have a-- a two-bedroom apartment in-- on the fourth floor, I believe. And, of course, I'm getting paid. Conditions are very poor, because it's after the war. And very little food, anyway. Everything is limited. Bread, you're getting according-- you're getting coupons or some other food. As free people-- everything on coupons. And the black market is 10 times as much as the regular price. The same thing happened even in the Soviet Union, when we were there-- when we were free. Also a black market. You know-- ripped some shoes-- you've got to pay an enormous amount of money. Yeah, yeah. And-- and then what happened-- I was there a few years in the-- in Waldenburg. Of course, [INAUDIBLE]. And then I found out that my brother also survived. And he was not far, that older brother, who was arrested at the same time as I was, was I-- when I told you in the beginning. He was not far from me. But what happened-- he was lucky. When he was arrested, he got his instruments as a watchmaker. And being in the camp, he was a watchmaker. Then he was like a king, like a prince, according to the conditions. Because they were-- for them, a [INAUDIBLE], a watch-- it was something like, here, a Rolls-Royce. And he used to fix them. And he used to get bread. And then I found out that my brother also-- you know, from other people-- that my brother is also in Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was near you in Komi, and  he wound up also near you in Waldenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also some place in Komi. But I didn't know, of course. No telephone, no communication, no-- no post office, no letters, nothing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I found out about him. He came here. He brought from the Soviet Union-- he was a watchmaker there. He brought some golden pieces, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people-- Russians-- they changed some coins, golden coins, for bread, for money. My brother was in good shape, because he was a watchmaker. And then we joined. And then what happened-- somebody-- and my brother used to deal with gold. And this was illegal. And somebody said to the police, and then he ran away from Waldenburg to Germany-- back to Germany, where it was DPs-- all the displaced persons. All those guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I couldn't then leave Poland. It started to get-- in the beginning, I didn't want to leave Poland, because I was so tired on those conditions in the Soviet Union that I felt in Waldenburg that I am in a-- in a-- in a-- in a hotel, you know? In good-- good conditions. But then I found out that my brother left from Germany-- left for the United States. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your other brother, in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your other brother, in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other brother got killed. I found no-- no trace. I tried, all those years, through the Red Cross, to find out about my parents. When I came back to Poland, I was trying to trace any trace about my-- I couldn't find anything. I wrote to Red Cross. Constantly, I'm getting letters-- they're trying, they're trying, they don't know. I wanted to find out at least where they were killed. Even-- a few years back, I didn't want to go back to Poland. I went just to be close to that-- to that-- mentally close to where they got killed. And also, I tried anything in Poland. I couldn't find out what happened-- not just to my parents, and all the family-- how they got killed, when they got killed. You see, in Jewish religion, there's Kaddish. Every-- the year-- when the-- the date when the people died, then you say a special-- pray Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't-- I didn't get any date. I-- I-- just even it is that holiday, Yom Kippur-- then I say Kaddish. [SIGHS] [DRINKS] Then, you see, being in Poland, I wanted to go to get united with my brother, because I didn't have no family. I was just the only one. And my brother-- I found out he's already in the United States. And then, you know, they let us register for Israel. And I registered myself. But because I was young and working for the government, they-- they refused me. Every year, they refused me. Because the elderly-- if I would be at this age now, or even 50 years old, they let those people out, because they wouldn't have any production from them. And young people, they refused. And what happened-- that I took a big risk. I have a friend who also was single and also wanted to leave, because he was by himself, and he wanted to join his family. But he was luckier than me, because he was a specialist in that, uh-- cooling, in the refrigeration. And he-- finally, he got a job at the border in Poland, where they-- the fishing, where they froze those-- those fish. Yeah. And he went there. And he said, I will try to get some connections and get from over there to Stockholm, to Sweden. And he used to-- came back to me. He said, Mark, listen. At that time, I was Majer. Listen-- no chance. Then he went back, but still was trying. And in the meantime, you know, I got a vacation, and I went to the mountains. When I came back, I-- somebody told me that he-- you see, over there, where he was, you couldn't go. You needed special permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he called to the-- where I used to live in Waldenburg to get the permission and to come to-- to his place. But when I came back, it was too late. And he was already in Sweden-- illegally, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But how this happened-- he got a Polack who was, you know, working in the post office and putting-- mail into the cars to Sweden. But he couldn't put him as the mail. But the Polish government used to export salt to Sweden. And he got an-- made an arrangement that he got some-- a lot of those blankets, and he put him into such-- car with salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was shipped in a carton of salt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And shipped. Yeah, he was shipped. And you can find out-- you can find out how I was feeling, that he was in Stockholm and I was here then.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was so nice to me. He used to write letters. But he was very careful, because even the-- Polish-- Poland became communist, they used to even check those letters, especially guys like me. They knew me, because I registered for Israel. It means I am also a criminal, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And little by little, he gave me to understand the name of that guy and to get in touch with him. But he gave it in a such way that he didn't put the full first name, not the full name, not the city. I got to think over. And finally, you know, I got-- I knew where to call. He used to work in a post office over there where he ran away. And then I was afraid even to make a long-distance call in the same town. Because if somebody would hear, they-- I would get into trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I left for a little town about 20 miles away, and I called up. I called him, and I am asking for this guy who took care on him. They say, what? He sits. In Poland, if you say 'sits,' which means he's in prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out. Look, it is my luck. He took me so much time to get this guy's name and the place where he lives. And finally he-- and what happened-- you know, like a Polack, he used to drink a lot. And when you drink, you talk. And probably he said something, and he-- he got into the prison. And I am still, I am working. The conditions are not so bad, you know. I am a free man. I'm writing letters to my brother, you know. He sends me some, you know, packages. The Jewish organization is sending in some, you know, some money. And I-- I was broken-- really, completely out. And then, you know, and I was trying different ways-- different ways to get-- to get a way out of Poland. I used to go to Warsaw and trying-- maybe I can, you know, to-- different ways, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day-- one day-- and I was working then, in Waldenburg, in a-- in a printing factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they wouldn't let in anybody into the office. The guy that, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's his name-- not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in front of the building, how you call this? Security guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: The doorman! The doorman wouldn't let-- yeah, that-- see? Plain word. I am nervous! I am completely nervous. And the doorman wouldn't-- wouldn't let-- And yet the doorman comes up and says, somebody wants to see you. And I came down. And in the meantime, I was trying very hard to get out of Poland. Different ways. You know, it would took me too much time to explain. And this guy tells me, you know, I have to talk to you about something. Then I said to him, listen, you have to wait. I have to work until 3 o'clock. We used to work there from 7 to 3 o'clock. At 3 o'clock, [INAUDIBLE] be downstairs. I come. And he was there. We went to a restaurant. And I offered him, you know, some vodka, and-- And-- and I was afraid to talk to him. Because I was thinking maybe, you know, the Communist Party's sending somebody to get something out of me to, you know. Because they considered me not a-- a good element, as a-- you know, as a-- suspicious. And I didn't-- he-- he didn't talk anything he wanted from me. I said-- I didn't know what to say. I said, you know, I am Mark Rosenthal. He said, you're Mark Rosenthal from [? Plock? ?] I said, yes. And then, when I looked at him-- this was just a few years-- I recognized this guy-- that I knew him. And he said-- [INAUDIBLE]. And I said to him, talk to me. He said, even now, if I would know, I cannot tell you anything, because I have one thing to do for you. I have to introduce you to somebody, and he will give you some help to get out of this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this man's name, who was helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- Borenstein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Borenstein, as I remember. And I remembered even his first name. But anyway, Borenstein. And, you know-- and, um-- where am I?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And he said to listen. Come to Warsaw. And I-- I got-- you know, I said to him, tell me something. He said, I don't know anything. I don't know anything. I have one thing to do. I have to get in touch with somebody-- with a very, very-- somebody-- with the-- And I came to Warsaw at the same time. He was there, that Borenstein. And guy-- a tall guy, good-looking. A diplomat. Later, I found out. And I got very scared. I turned around. The Borenstein is not there. I am just with this man. And he-- we went a few blocks. He took me to a little cafeteria. He took out a letter, you know, and I recognized my friend from Stockholm. I say, that is Yakov Babad! His name is Babad. B-A-B-A-D. And Yakov-- Yakov. And he was very happy, because he understood that he got the right guy. Because he was very-- very-- he felt very bad in Stockholm that he accomplished, and I was left in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this friend's name-- the one who wound up in Stockholm?

speaker: interviewee
text: That guy-- the diplomat?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the one who wound up in Stockholm.

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Oh, his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakov Babad, the friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Babad, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And this guy was-- the diplomat-- he was very happy, because he knew right away if I said Yakov Babad, I recognized his handwriting. And he said, you know, it's not so easy. I don't promise you anything. He want-- he came to the consulate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He begged me for help. He felt very uncomfortable that he is in-- in Sweden, and I am still in Poland. But this guy-- that-- don't lose, don't lose any-- any hope. We will do something. And, you know, he said-- you know, people like me, got refuse-- the government refused them to go to Israel. And he said to me-- he-- first of all, he gave me money. He said to me, first of all, don't say to anybody that you're meeting me. Because to me, nothing will happen. I can be persona non grata. And they will tell me, leave-- leave Poland and go back to your-- to your country. But you-- you will go into the prison, and you'll never came out. And be very careful. And we will meet each other very often. But all the time, look all around that nobody is after you. And after a few times, he said to me, you know, Mark, give me all the names of those people who wanted to go to Israel. Give me their names and addresses. I gave him the addresses. What happened, you see-- in Poland is a such bank-- like, they call it PKO-- that you're getting dollars, but you don't get the dollars. You get, in exchange, merchandise. And-- and if you sell the merchandise, you can-- let's say $25, you can help yourself, like a single man, for two months, at that time, in 1940-- in 1954. And-- and, you know, and I'm working-- like, we used to meet all these people who put in for Israel. Because we knew each other. We knew that's our destination. And somebody comes to me. You know, Mark, I got from PKO $25. I don't know-- and it came from Paris. I said, you know, I don't know. I got also, and I don't know what happened. It's a small town, where I used to live. I go there. Mark, you know, I got $25. I said, I got, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: But from whom? How it happened? I knew that's because I gave the address.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all this went through a few months. And finally, once, I came to him. He said to me, Mark, next time, bring me three photos-- for the passport photos. And be very careful. And he said to me-- no-- in the-- in [INAUDIBLE], he asked me many times, I give you so much money. Try to get your passport. It's very hard. But I couldn't do it. You know, it's a communist system. I tried different ways-- no way. I'm telling it very short, because it's a very long, long story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, he tells me, bring the-- I brought the pictures, you know? Then he said, come on this-and-this date, but leave everything at your apartment. Don't say anything. Don't sell anything from the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't give to anybody your keys. Be very careful. You know where you are. I said, I know. And-- I am getting excited. That's-- that makes me a little bit nervous, and I cannot-- the way I would like to tell it the real way. And what happened-- I came. I came. He said, next time you come, bring me your-- your documentations, all your money, Polish money. And you will get the same amount of money in Israel. Yes. And the next time I came to him-- yeah-- I came to him, I met him in front of some little hotel. He gave me a package. And he said, Mark, everything is there. And he said-- no-- he said-- before, you're getting any packages. When you will come for-- be dressed a little bit like, uh-- not from Poland. I used to have those American, you know, suits, the ties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't look like a Polack. Look like a little-- you know? And that's the way I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came. He gave me a package. And he said, you know, you're having-- you're getting-- inside is an Austrian passport, a diplomatic passport. You left this morning-- 6 o'clock this morning, you left-- you left Vienna. And you have to stay in Warsaw, because the other train, which goes to Stockholm, goes away 7 o'clock PM. And inside-- he said, give me all your money. And leave yourself a little money for the whole-- for the few hours, to live up-- to live up. And I was only by myself, a little bit nervous. I don't remember. I went to the-- Central Park, let's say, like here. I open. A magazine, an Austrian magazine from two days back. Chocolate, Austrian chocolate. A razor, Austrian razor. You know, bread-- cut bread like we never knew. Salami. Yeah. And I see the passport in my-- in not my name. I am Katz-- Richard Katz on the passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-- R-I-C-H-A-R-D. Richard. And last name Katz-- K-A-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I shouldn't say it, you know? But it's so many years back that-- not a big problem. And and I-- you know, I see everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so many visas in the passport. Unbelievable! This guy who used to have before me, with a different picture, used to travel a lot. And also, you know, the visa to East Germany was a little bit, you know-- it was an error in spelling. Because in Russian, they-- they put the date when the visa, not just like a check. You have to write the date, the number, even in words.
